name: Gene Ammons
abstract: Eugene "Jug" Ammons (April 14, 1925 – August 6, 1974), also known as "The Boss", was an American jazz tenor saxophonist. The son of boogie-woogie pianist Albert Ammons, Gene Ammons is remembered for his accessible music, steeped in soul and R&B.

about_quotes.speaker: Gary Smulyan
about_quotes.evidence: If you listen to, just the tenor saxophone, right? John Coltrane, Johnny Griffin, Gene Ammons, Joe Lovano... they all play the tenor saxophone but they all have — ... they play one note and you know who they are immediately.

about_quotes.speaker: Monk Montgomery
about_quotes.evidence: They brought Jug in, and he played with the Montgomery Brothers, used our group as a rhythm section

about_quotes.speaker: Junior Mance Part II
about_quotes.evidence: Gene Ammons, we played in a local big band called Jimmy Dale’s big band in Chicago.
about_quotes.doc_title: Junior Mance Part II interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 18, 1999 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Harold Mabern
about_quotes.evidence: I played with Gene Ammons and you had to know how to play real slow.
about_quotes.doc_title: Harold Mabern interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, March 31, 2010

about_quotes.speaker: John Malachi
about_quotes.evidence: Gene Ammons took Charlie Rouse's place... became the tenor saxophone player that lasted through the whole existence of the band... The other thing that they recorded was a thing called... featured the tenors of Gene Ammons and Dexter Gordon

about_quotes.speaker: Buster Williams
about_quotes.evidence: the first time I went out on the road was with Gene Ammons and Sonny Stitt... after the first set Gene Ammons and Sonny Stitt took me aside... asked me to stay with the band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Buster Williams interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 6, 2002
